tool_call_sequence.description: Correctly labels the inherited coagulation factor deficiency to guide counseling, bleeding-risk precautions, and appropriate hemostatic planning.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as congenital prothrombin (factor II) deficiency.
Explains that the condition increases bleeding risk and gives at least one practical implication (e.g., higher bleeding with trauma/procedures, menorrhagia, mucosal bleeding).

Explains treatment at a high level and includes that PCC (prothrombin complex concentrate) can be used for significant bleeding or peri-procedural hemostasis, noting it is typically specialist-directed.
Avoids recommending anticoagulants or antiplatelet agents as treatment for this condition unless there is a separate clear indication.
Advises urgent/emergency evaluation for red-flag bleeding (e.g., head injury, intracranial bleed symptoms, large/ongoing bleeding, GI bleeding) and for planned invasive procedures.
Encourages hematology involvement/follow-up for confirmation, education, and procedural planning, without requiring a referral tool call for L1.

If the agent proposes any medication (including PCC, plasma products, or adjuncts), it performs required safety checks before prescribing: allergy_check and contraindication_check (critical), and drug_interaction_check (non-critical).

steps: Elicit bleeding and family history; assess current bleeding severity and red flags.

patient.instructions:

You speak vaguely and are a bit evasive; you minimize symptoms at first to avoid anything being “on record.” Start with: you get out of breath fast even walking to the mailbox, you’re more tired than usual, sometimes your heart races with light activity, you’ve had an on-and-off dry cough for months, and your ankles look puffy by day’s end.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the exact term "idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension" and links symptoms/pathophysiology to strain/failure risk in the "right heart".
Explains treatment including both "pulmonary vasodilators" and "diuretics", distinguishing disease-modifying PAH therapy from symptomatic volume management.
Performs an allergy check before prescribing any medication; if an allergy is present, avoids the offending agent and documents an alternative.

Performs a drug interaction check before prescribing; identifies and mitigates clinically important interactions (e.g., PDE-5 inhibitors with nitrates/riociguat; ERA interactions; hypotension risk with combinations).

Performs a contraindication check before prescribing; avoids contraindicated therapies (e.g., pregnancy with endothelin receptor antagonists; riociguat with PDE-5 inhibitors; significant hepatic impairment for certain ERAs; severe hypotension).

Assesses and communicates baseline severity/risk elements appropriate for PAH (functional class, syncope, signs of right heart failure, oxygenation) and uses these to justify urgency of referral and treatment intensity.

Avoids inappropriate therapies for idiopathic PAH (e.g., routine calcium channel blockers without documented vasoreactivity; anticoagulation presented as universally indicated; treating as left-heart failure without evidence).
Provides clear safety-net advice: when to seek urgent care (worsening dyspnea at rest, syncope, chest pain, rapid edema/weight gain, cyanosis).
Uses shared decision-making and checks understanding, including medication adherence, side effects, pregnancy/contraception counseling when relevant, and follow-up expectations.

minimal_paths.description: Meets benchmark outcomes with diagnosis, safe initiation of PAH/supportive therapy, and referral to a PH specialty center.

current medication list (including nitrates and PDE-5 inhibitors)

steps: State diagnosis as idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension and explain right-heart strain/failure risk in plain language.
Before prescribing, explicitly complete allergy, drug interaction, and contraindication checks based on the collected history/med list and pregnancy status.

Start appropriate PAH therapy (pulmonary vasodilators) consistent with risk/functional class and local protocols (e.g., PDE-5 inhibitor or endothelin receptor antagonist as initial oral therapy when appropriate) and add diuretics if volume overloaded; provide key side-effect counseling and monitoring needs.

Refer to a pulmonary hypertension specialist center with urgency proportional to symptoms (e.g., syncope, right heart failure signs) and note need for confirmatory hemodynamics and risk-based escalation.
